title: Each storage copy serves a distinct role: local reads, multi-device sync, or self-sovereign recovery
content:
Not every copy of the data plays the same role. Evaluating duplication requires understanding what each copy is for:

| Copy | Where | Purpose | Can recover from it alone? |
|---|---|---|:---:|
| PGlite | Browser | Fast local reads, UI rendering | No (ephemeral) |
| PostgreSQL | Switchboard | Multi-device sync, team collaboration, server-side processing | Yes (authoritative) |
| Swarm /bytes | Decentralized network | Censorship-resistant backup, serverless recovery, wallet-based access | Yes (self-sovereign) |

**Key points**:
- PGlite is **not** a recovery store — it's a performance layer. Losing it is annoying but not catastrophic as long as a remote exists.
- PostgreSQL and Swarm are both recovery-complete, but with different trust models: PostgreSQL requires a server operator, Swarm requires only a wallet signature.
- Switchboard supports server-side processing (document models, auth, etc.); Swarm alone does not.
- Swarm is censorship-resistant in ways PostgreSQL cannot be.

**Consequence**: "duplicate" is the wrong frame for comparing Switchboard and Swarm. They are **alternative recovery stores** with different trust and capability profiles. Only when both run simultaneously (Architecture C) does the word "duplicate" genuinely apply — and even then the motivation is redundancy, not waste.
description: PGlite (fast reads, not recoverable alone), PostgreSQL (multi-device, server-side), Swarm /bytes (censorship-resistant, wallet-based) — each copy has its own recovery role.
